digest.top_line: PEACE-2: pelvic RT missed cPFS primary (HR 0.81, p=0.088); POP-RT benefit not replicated in PSMA-PET-staged very high-risk PCa.
digest.tldr: PEACE-2 challenges the emerging pelvic nodal RT standard in PSMA-PET-staged high-risk PCa; prostate SBRT safety data (PIVOTALboost, PACE-NODES, PRIME) matures across risk strata. Breast carried the day's heaviest load: 10-15yr hypofractionation trials confirm durable equivalence, and EORTC IM-MS at 20yr quantifies the late non-BC mortality cost of IM field RT.

sites.intro: ESTRO26 prostate session anchored on pelvic nodal RT (benefit unproven with modern PSMA-PET staging) alongside SBRT safety readouts across high-risk and oligorecurrent settings.

studies.name: PIVOTALboost
studies.tldr: PIVOTALboost phase 3: focal boost + pelvic node RT (20fr) safe at 2yr; early bowel toxicity with nodal RT resolves by 18wks.
details: 🔍 Phase 3 RCT, N=1465, 39 UK centres, high-risk localised prostate; 4 arms: prostate IMRT alone, +boost, +pelvic nodes+boost; 1° EP biochemical/clinical failure (oncologic results not reported here)
📊 Late G2+ AEs at 2yr (973 pts with ≥2yr f/u)
📊 Early bowel toxicity elevated with pelvic node RT but resolved by 18wks post-RT; no G3+ signal persisting
⚠️ Only side-effect endpoints reported at ESTRO26; 1° EP (biochemical/clinical failure) not yet presented — efficacy read pending
🔗 Focal boost arm consistent with prior FLAME/DELINEATE data showing no excess toxicity with dose-escalated boost to dominant intraprostatic lesion

verdict.rationale: Toxicity-only interim read; 1° efficacy endpoint (BCF) not yet reported. Safety reassuring but trial incomplete.
verdict.audience: High-risk localised prostate, 20-fraction IMRT, ± focal boost ± pelvic nodal RT

open_questions: Does focal boost improve biochemical/clinical failure vs prostate-only RT?
Does pelvic nodal RT add efficacy benefit to offset early bowel toxicity?

studies.name: RADIOSA
studies.tldr: RADIOSA post-hoc: SBRT+ADT vs SBRT alone, median MFS 16.6mo vs NR, HR 0.39 (p=0.001) in oligorecurrent prostate.
details: 🔍 Phase II RCT, N=102 (1:1); SBRT alone vs SBRT + 6-month ADT; oligorecurrent PCa; median f/u 49.23mo (95% CI 42.47-54.8)
📊 Eugonadal MFS (post-testosterone recovery) also favored Arm B, p<0.05; all but 2 Arm B pts recovered testosterone within f/u
⚠️ Post-hoc analysis; MFS was not the prespecified primary endpoint of RADIOSA; phase II, N=102 limits definitiveness
⚠️ MFS as OS surrogate in oligorecurrent PCa is less validated than in M0 non-metastatic settings; clinical extrapolation uncertain
🔗 Short-term ADT + SBRT benefit pattern directionally consistent with ORIOLE/STOMP oligomet-directed RT data, though those trials did not include ADT

verdict.rationale: Post-hoc analysis of phase II RCT; MFS not the prespecified primary endpoint; N=102 too small for practice-level inference.
verdict.audience: Oligorecurrent hormone-sensitive prostate cancer, SBRT candidates

open_questions: Does eugonadal MFS advantage translate to OS benefit in oligorecurrent PCa?
Optimal ADT duration combined with SBRT (6mo vs shorter vs none)?
Phase III confirmation needed for SBRT+ADT as oligorecurrent standard

studies.name: PACE-NODES
studies.tldr: PACE-NODES acute tox interim: PPN-SBRT raises CTCAE ≥2 GI events (28% vs 21% at 12wk) but resolves; no GU difference; cancer control endpoint immature.
details: 📊 GI excess transient: EPIC-26 bowel domain worse at 4 weeks; no CTCAE ≥2 GI difference at 12-week endpoint
📊 CTCAE ≥2 GU: no difference, clinician or patient-reported
🔍 Phase 3 RCT, N=1166, high-risk PCa (T3a-T4 and/or GS 8-10 and/or PSA >20), 12-36mo ADT; 36.25Gy/5f prostate ± 25Gy/5f pelvic nodes on alternate days
⚠️ 11% PPN-SBRT vs 4% P-SBRT did not receive allocated treatment (constraints unmet); primary BCF endpoint not yet mature, cancer control data pending
🔗 Pelvic nodal RT benefit in high-risk PCa established with conventional/moderate hypofractionation (POP-RT, PEACE-2); PACE-NODES is first phase 3 testing SBRT delivery at scale

verdict.rationale: Interim safety analysis only; primary BCF endpoint immature. No cancer control data yet to inform SOC.
verdict.audience: High-risk localised PCa (T3a-T4, GS 8-10, or PSA >20), 12-36mo ADT planned

open_questions: Whether BCF benefit of pelvic nodal RT translates when delivered as SBRT
Late GI/GU toxicity (≥2 yr) with pelvic nodal SBRT

studies.name: PRIME
studies.tldr: PRIME interim: 5-fx SBRT with pelvic RT and ADT comparable to moderate hypofractionation on early BFFS in high-risk/node-positive PCa; mature data pending.
details: 🔍 Phase III non-inferiority RCT, N~434, high-risk/very high-risk/node-positive non-metastatic PCa; Tata Memorial Centre; accrual 2018-2023; median f/u 1-2yr (interim)
💊 Arm A: 36.25 Gy/5 fx every other day; Arm B: ~68 Gy/25 fx (2.7 Gy/fx); pelvic nodes 25 Gy/5 fx; long-course ADT ~2yr both arms
📊 1° EP (BFFS, Phoenix nadir+2): no inferiority signal for SBRT at interim; no effect size in source; 4-5yr mature results pending

details.text: 🔗 vs HYPO-RT-PC (10-yr mature): adjusted HR 0.84 (95% CI 0.69-1.03), non-inferiority confirmed; key design differences
subdetails: HYPO-RT-PC: intermediate-to-high-risk, node-negative only, NO ADT, no pelvic RT
PRIME: high-risk/node-positive included, mandatory 2yr ADT, whole-pelvis RT in both arms
PRIME is first RCT extending 5-fx SBRT to the nodal/higher-risk ADT context

details: ⚠️ Interim data only (1-2yr); no HR/CI for BFFS primary endpoint; non-inferiority conclusion requires 4-5yr analysis; all efficacy currently trend-level

verdict.rationale: Interim analysis at 1-2yr; primary non-inferiority endpoint (BFFS) not yet mature; 4-5yr results required to confirm.
verdict.audience: High-risk, very high-risk, or node-positive non-metastatic PCa, ECOG 0-2

open_questions: Will BFFS non-inferiority hold at 4-5yr mature analysis?
Late toxicity trajectory with longer follow-up?
Generalizability beyond Indian academic centers with PSMA-staged disease?

studies.name: PEACE-2
studies.tldr: PEACE-2: pelvic RT + 3yr ADT vs prostate-only RT missed primary cPFS endpoint (HR 0.81, p=0.088) in very high-risk localized PCa at interim.
details: 🔍 Phase III randomized, pelvic vs prostate-only RT, very high-risk PCa (≥2 of Gleason ≥8, T3-T4, PSA ≥20), N0M0, 3yr ADT + 78Gy IMRT; N~761, f/u ~5.5yr

details.text: 🔗 POP-RT vs PEACE-2: WPRT benefit diverges starkly across imaging eras and ADT duration

details: ⚠️ Interim analysis (ESTRO 2026); OS not reported. Modern PSMA PET/CT staging + longer ADT may have neutralized incremental pelvic RT benefit vs conventional-imaging POP-RT era.
⚠️ Presenter challenges 'very high-risk' label: <1/10 men died from PCa in first decade, especially with PSMA PET/CT filtering occult nodal disease pre-enrollment.

verdict.rationale: Interim analysis at ESTRO 2026; prespecified final primary cPFS + OS analysis pending. Cannot draw definitive conclusion on WPRT benefit yet.
verdict.audience: Very high-risk localized PCa, ≥2 of Gleason ≥8/T3-T4/PSA ≥20, N0M0

open_questions: OS outcome at final analysis; will null cPFS translate?
Whether POP-RT vs PEACE-2 divergence reflects staging era, ADT duration, or RT dose
Biomarkers to select pts still benefiting from pelvic RT in modern-staged disease

sites.intro: EXTEND RCT publishes in JCO; OligoCare registry quantifies SBRT local control across 2447 pts and 3533 lesions, flagging CRC as the outlier histology.

studies.name: EXTEND
studies.tldr: EXTEND phase II RCT: MDT added to SOC for oligometastatic solid tumors; primary analysis now published in JCO.
details: 🔍 Phase II randomized, multi-histology basket design; MDT (SBRT/SABR) + SOC vs SOC alone across oligometastatic solid tumor histologies
📄 Primary aggregated analysis published JCO May 16 2026 (DOI: 10.1200/JCO-25-02856); ctDNA correlatives presented synchronously at ESTRO26
⚠️ No effect sizes reported in source tweet; primary endpoint results not extractable from available OCR
🔗 Adds to MDT-in-oligomets RCT body alongside SABR-COMET, ORIOLE, STOMP; histology-basket design allows cross-tumor signal detection but dilutes site-specific conclusions
❓ ctDNA correlatives presented at ESTRO26 may identify biomarker-selected pts most likely to benefit from MDT

verdict.rationale: Phase II randomized basket trial; primary endpoint effect sizes not in source. Adds signal to oligomets MDT literature but underpowered for definitive conclusions per histology.
verdict.audience: Oligometastatic solid tumors, multiple histologies, 1-5 mets, MDT-eligible

open_questions: Which histology baskets drove the aggregated benefit?
Do ctDNA correlatives identify MDT responders prospectively?
Confirmatory phase III needed per histology or aggregated design sufficient?

studies.name: OligoCare
studies.tldr: OligoCare registry: SBRT 88.6% LC at 3yr across 2447 oligomet pts; CRC worst at 19.6% local progression at 3yr.
details: 📊 Overall local in-field progression: 5.0% at 1yr, 11.4% at 3yr (88.6% LC); 2447 pts, 3533 lesions, 57 institutions, median f/u 31mo
🔍 EORTC prospective multi-institution registry, July 2019-July 2025; interim analysis, not a randomised trial
📐 Minimum PTV dose strongest technical predictor of local control across the dataset
🔗 De novo OMD: better LC than repeat OMD, attributed to higher delivered dose achievable at first treatment
⚠️ CRC highest local failure (19.6% at 3yr) despite receiving highest median dose per fraction; relative radioresistance flagged, combination or dose-escalation strategies proposed

verdict.rationale: Prospective registry, single-arm, no randomised comparator; interim analysis. Large real-world N validates feasibility but cannot shift SOC.
verdict.audience: Oligometastatic solid tumor, all histologies, SBRT candidates

open_questions: Optimal dose/combination strategy for CRC mets given apparent radioresistance
Minimum PTV dose threshold to define technical adequacy standards
Whether de novo vs repeat OMD LC advantage persists after dose-matched analysis

sites.intro: Breast dominated numerically: 10-15yr RCT datasets across hypofractionation, APBI, IM-MS RT, boost indication, and oligomet SABR converge at the same meeting.

studies.name: OLIGOMA
studies.tldr: OLIGOMA: SABR to all mets + systemic vs systemic alone for OMD breast, mPFS 35.8 vs 20.4mo, HR 0.48 (0.25-0.91), p=0.021.
details: 🔍 Phase II RCT (ARO-2021-09), N=87; OMD breast any line, ≤5 mets; SABR to all lesions; co-primary PFS + QoL at 12wks
💊 Concomitant: endocrine 67%, CDK4/6i 49%, HER2-targeted 12%; ER+ ~77%; bone-dominant mets (65%); median f/u 26.0mo
⚠️ Baseline imbalance: synchronous mets 54% (MDT) vs 39% (control); single-met pts 54% vs 41%; may inflate MDT arm benefit
⚠️ N=87, wide CI (0.25-0.91); QoL co-primary not reported in source; OS immature
🔗 vs SINDAS (Zheng, JAMA Oncol 2020): prior phase 2 RCT in OMD breast, SBRT positive; OLIGOMA adds CDK4/6i backbone (~49%)

verdict.rationale: Randomised phase II, N=87; wide CI (0.25-0.91). Data still maturing (upper CI not reached). Baseline imbalance in synchronous mets (54% vs 39%) and QoL co-primary unreported.
verdict.audience: Oligometastatic breast cancer, any treatment line, ≤5 mets, any receptor subtype

open_questions: Does OS benefit accrue at longer follow-up?
QoL co-primary result: does MDT preserve or impair quality of life?
Optimal selection: synchronous vs metachronous, single vs multiple mets?

studies.name: HypoG-01
studies.tldr: HypoG-01 secondary analysis: failure patterns similar between 40Gy/15fx and 50Gy/25fx; LRR only 16% of 118 events, 67% in-volume.
details: 🔍 Pre-planned secondary ITT analysis of HypoG-01 phase III; N=1,260, median f/u 4.8yr; randomised 40Gy/15fx vs 50Gy/25fx + tumour-bed boost
📊 118 first events: IDR 51% (n=61), second malignancy 31% (n=37), LRR 16% (n=20); LRR lowest-frequency category
📊 Of LRR sites (n=30): 20/30 (67%) in-volume; 19/30 nodal, mainly L1/L2 levels
🔗 Failure patterns not obviously different between 3-week and 5-week arms -- supports hypofractionation equivalence in failure distribution
🔗 In-volume predominance of LRR supports adequacy of ESTRO contouring guidelines for target-volume definition
⚠️ Secondary analysis only; arm-specific LRR rates and HR/p not reported in source; primary HypoG-01 efficacy data not presented here
studies.key_figure_caption: 118 first events: IDR 51% (n=61), second malignancy 31% (n=37), LRR 16% (n=20). LRR sites (n=30): 20/30 (67%) in-volume; 19/30 nodal.

verdict.rationale: Pre-planned secondary phase III analysis; failure patterns comparable between arms, consistent with FAST-Forward hypofractionation equivalence data.
verdict.audience: Early breast cancer, adjuvant RT post-surgery

open_questions: Arm-specific LRR rates not reported; does boost schedule modulate in-volume control?
Long-term (>5yr) distant recurrence trajectory by arm

studies.name: DBCG HYPO
studies.tldr: DBCG HYPO 10-yr: 40 Gy/15f reduced G2-3 induration (19.5% vs 24.7%, HR 0.76) with equivalent recurrence and survival at 12.8yr median f/u.
details: 🔍 Phase III RCT (1:1), N=1,882 node-negative BC/DCIS, 50 Gy/25f vs 40 Gy/15f, Denmark/Norway/Germany, 2009-2014; median f/u 12.8yr
📊 No significant differences in locoregional recurrence, distant failure, or breast cancer mortality at 10yr
🔗 Adds longest randomized f/u dataset (12.8yr) to prior hypofractionation evidence (START B, FAST-Forward), confirming durable toxicity and oncologic equivalence
⚠️ 1° EP was 3-yr induration, not 10-yr; 10-yr oncologic outcomes are prespecified secondary analyses

verdict.rationale: Phase III RCT, 12.8yr median f/u; hypofractionation already global SOC. Confirms durable toxicity benefit without oncologic compromise.
verdict.audience: Node-negative early breast cancer or DCIS, adjuvant whole-breast RT

open_questions: Whether ultra-hypofractionation (5fr) shows similar long-term induration reduction vs 15fr
Applicability to pts requiring regional nodal irradiation or post-mastectomy RT

studies.name: Dutch Breast Boost Indication Study
studies.tldr: 10yr IBTR 1.2% with or without boost in 0-2 risk factor pts; boost likely omittable in modern-era BCS.
details: 🔍 Retrospective Dutch registry, BCT + WBRT 2012-2016; IBTR via pathology report algorithm; Assisi thresholds: omit boost if 10yr IBTR <3% with boost, <6% without
🔍 Risk factors: age ≤40, grade 3, TNBC, inadequate guideline systemic therapy, no pCR after NAC (TNBC/HER2+)
📊 Assisi threshold exceeded only in ≥3 RF boost arm at 10yr (3.3% > 3% threshold); all no-boost groups below 6%
⚠️ Non-randomized; ≥3 RF no-boost arm N=149 vs N=733 on boost — selection bias likely drives artificially low IBTR in pts forgoing boost
🔗 EORTC 22881/10882 (Bartelink, Lancet Oncol 2015): boost cut IBTR ~50% in 2000s era; absolute rates substantially lower with modern systemic therapy

verdict.rationale: Retrospective non-randomized registry; selection bias in boost allocation undermines causal inference needed to justify boost omission.
verdict.audience: Early breast cancer, BCS + whole-breast RT, 0-2 high-risk features, modern syste

open_questions: Randomized confirmation needed before boost omission for 0-2 RF pts in routine practice
Boost benefit in ≥3 RF pts with modern targeted agents (CDK4/6i, olaparib, T-DM1)?
pCR after NAC as reliable gate for boost omission in TNBC/HER2+ pts?

studies.name: IMPORT HIGH
studies.tldr: IMPORT HIGH 10-yr: IBTR 3.7% (48Gy SIB) vs 3.5% sequential boost; 53Gy at 5.5%; 48Gy SIB reaffirmed as SOC.
details: 🔍 Randomised 1:1:1, N=2617, 76 UK hospitals 2009-2015; pT1-3, pN0-N3a, M0 post-BCS requiring tumour bed boost; annual f/u to 10 years
📐 OS at 10 years: absolute diff vs 40Gy/15F: -0.5% (-3.0, 2.8) for 48Gy; +1.5% (-1.4, 5.1) for 53Gy

details.text: 📊 Late AEs at 10 years (moderate/marked, similar across all arms)

details: 🔗 5-yr IBTR (Lancet 2023): 1.9%/2.0%/3.2% (40+16/48/53Gy); 10-yr rates roughly double but rank order preserved, 48Gy non-inferiority sustained
⚠️ No HR reported for IBTR comparison; absolute differences only. 53Gy numerically worse IBTR than control at 10 years; further dose escalation not beneficial.

verdict.rationale: Mature 10-yr RCT follow-up confirming 5-yr non-inferiority (Lancet 2023); 48Gy SIB durable, safe, and explicitly endorsed as SOC.
verdict.audience: Early BC (pT1-3, pN0-N3a, M0) post-BCS requiring tumour bed boost

open_questions: Whether 53Gy higher IBTR (5.5% vs 3.5%) reflects true dose-harm or statistical noise
Breast cosmesis trajectory beyond 5 years (PRO data collected to 5 years only)
Generalizability in era of genomic risk stratification (enrolled 2009-2015)

studies.name: RAPCHEM
studies.tldr: RAPCHEM 10-yr data: response-adapted locoregional RT de-escalation post-NAC yields <3% recurrence rates in breast.
details: 📊 10-yr locoregional recurrence <3% with nodal-response-guided RT de-escalation post-neoadjuvant chemo
🔍 Response-adapted approach: locoregional RT field tailored to nodal response after NAC; 10-yr follow-up reported at ESTRO 2026
🔗 vs B-51/NSABP: complete RT omission post-NAC still under prospective validation; RAPCHEM data supports de-escalation but not elimination
⚠️ No effect sizes beyond recurrence rate <3% reported in source tweet; full endpoint table not available
⚠️ Tweet in Spanish; no NCT, N, or HR/CI data in source; numeric granularity limited

verdict.rationale: 10-yr mature data supporting response-adapted RT de-escalation, consistent with emerging practice; complete RT omission remains unvalidated.
verdict.audience: Early/locally advanced breast, node-positive, post-neoadjuvant chemo with nodal

open_questions: Does complete RT omission post-pCR remain safe? (B-51 pending)
Which nodal response threshold defines safe de-escalation?

studies.name: EORTC IM-MS (22922/10925)
studies.tldr: EORTC IM-MS 20 yrs: IM-MS RT reduces BC mortality (HR 0.82) but excess non-BC deaths (HR 1.26) yields null OS (HR 1.00, P=0.967).

details.text: ⚠️ RT-related late effects at 20 yrs (IM-MS RT vs no IM-MS RT, absolute rates)

details: 🔍 Phase III randomized, N=4004, stage I-III BC; EORTC 22922/10925; Lancet Oncol in press
⚠️ Non-BC mortality excess separates after ~15 yrs (Δ4.6%); era IM fields implicated — presenter noted modern cardiac-sparing RT may alter this
⚠️ pN0 subset published separately in Lancet Oncol as unplanned analysis; read with caution

verdict.rationale: Randomised phase III, 20-yr primary OS null (HR 1.00); BCM benefit fully offset by excess non-BC deaths from late RT toxicity.
verdict.audience: Stage I-III breast cancer, nodal-positive or medial/central tumor location

open_questions: Does modern cardiac-sparing IM-MS RT (IMRT, protons) avoid the non-BC mortality excess?
Which subgroup retains net OS benefit at 20 yrs: nodal-positive, medial tumors, pN0?

studies.name: APBI-IMRT Florence
studies.tldr: APBI-IMRT Florence 15yr: IBTR 7.7% vs 4.2% (HR 1.57, p=0.17); no difference in locoregional control, OS, or DM at 15 years.
details: 🔍 Phase III RCT N=520 (2005-2013); APBI 30Gy/5# IMRT vs WBI 50Gy/25# + 10Gy TBB; BCS, pT<25mm, FSM≥5mm, age>40
📊 15-yr IBTR excess driven by new ipsilateral primaries (APBI 5.9% vs WBI 2.7%, p=0.09); true local relapse equivalent (2.1% vs 1.6%, p=0.75)
🔗 Consistent with RAPID (NEJM 2019) and IMPORT LOW (Lancet 2021) at 10yr; Florence now provides the most mature phase III APBI data
⚠️ Equivalence trial powered for 5-yr IBTR; HR 1.57 (0.82-3.04) upper CI doesn't exclude meaningful long-term excess; p=0.17 is non-significant but not equivalence-demonstrated

verdict.rationale: Phase III RCT, 15yr f/u, primary endpoint non-significant; no OS or DM differences; supports APBI in selected early BC per ASTRO/ESTRO guidelines.
verdict.audience: Early BC post-BCS, pT<25mm, FSM≥5mm, age>40

open_questions: Whether new ipsilateral primaries reflect inadequate PBI volume coverage vs independent field biology
Long-term cosmesis and patient-reported outcomes at 15yr
Applicability with modern surgical margins and hypofractionated WBI as comparator

studies.name: DBCG RT Natural
studies.tldr: DBCG RT Natural: PBI 40Gy/15fr cut 4-yr LR to 1.5% vs 9.8% (no RT) in low-risk BCS; phase III interim.
details: 📊 +RT 1.5% (0.3-5.1%) vs -RT 9.8% (5.9-14.9%) 4-yr CIF; 39 of 41 LR events in no-PBI arms; -RT crossed the pre-specified 4% threshold
🔍 Phase III RCT; ≥60yr, pT1N0, ER≥10%, HER2-normal, grade 1-2, post-BCS margin ≥2mm; PBI 40Gy/15fr; n≈800; stratified by institution + ET; median f/u 4yr
🔗 vs PRIME II / CALGB 9343: ET-alone showed ~2-4% 5yr LR in low-risk elderly pts; -RT+ET here 3.7% consistent; RT alone (3.0%) ≈ ET alone (3.7%)
⚠️ Primary EP is 5-yr invasive LR; first results at median 4yr; ET adherence heterogeneous (≥4.5yr threshold definition); open-label by necessity
🔗 EUROPA (per commentator): RT superior QoL vs ET; DBCG Natural supports RT as effective ET-equivalent for local control

verdict.rationale: Phase III RCT with prespecified primary EP; confirms PBI benefit in low-risk BCS; consistent with PRIME II/CALGB 9343 direction; interim at 4yr.
verdict.audience: ≥60yr pT1N0, ER≥10%, HER2-normal, grade 1-2 breast cancer, post-BCS

open_questions: Whether RT or ET is the preferred single-modality adjuvant given similar LR rates
Full 5-yr primary endpoint readout to confirm threshold non-exceedance

studies.name: DIREKHT
studies.tldr: DIREKHT: de-intensification in post-op HNSCC via contralateral neck sparing and/or primary CTV dose reduction to 56 Gy.
details: 🔍 Post-operative RT de-intensification trial in HNSCC; interventions: contralateral neck sparing and/or primary CTV dose reduction to 56 Gy (vs standard ~60-66 Gy)
⚠️ No effect sizes, endpoints, or outcomes reported in source tweet; detailed results not available from this tweet alone
🔗 Fits broader de-escalation movement in post-op HNSCC alongside ECOG-ACRIN 3311 and PATHOS, which target similar RT volume/dose reduction in HPV+ and low-risk pts
❓ Whether contralateral neck sparing alone vs dose reduction vs combination confers the greatest toxicity reduction without compromising locoregional control

verdict.rationale: Source tweet previews trial concept only; no outcomes, endpoints, N, or effect sizes reported. Cannot classify without results.
verdict.audience: Post-op HNSCC, eligible for de-intensified adjuvant RT (contralateral neck low-r

open_questions: Primary endpoint and oncologic outcomes not reported in source
Which de-intensification strategy (neck sparing vs dose reduction vs both) drives toxicity benefit?
Applicability to HPV- vs HPV+ subgroups

studies.name: RCC SBRT (5-year local control)
studies.tldr: SBRT for primary RCC: 100% local control at 5 years per tweet report; no effect sizes in source.
details: 📊 1° EP: 100% local control at 5 years reported in tweet; no numeric CI, HR, or N in source
🔍 SBRT for primary renal cell carcinoma; fractionation schedule, N, and eligibility not reported in source tweets
🔗 Consistent with prior single-institution SBRT-RCC series (FASTRACK II: 100% LC at 2yr, N=70); 5-yr follow-up extends maturity of that signal
⚠️ Tweet only; no abstract, paper, or slide with denominator, confidence intervals, AE data, or comparator arm
⚠️ Single-arm design by context; no randomised comparator vs partial nephrectomy or active surveillance

verdict.rationale: Single-arm, no comparator, no source document beyond a tweet. 100% LC consistent with prior series but unverifiable without paper.
verdict.audience: Primary RCC, inoperable or surveillance-eligible, treated with SBRT

open_questions: N, fractionation, and pt eligibility criteria not reported in source
Renal function outcomes and late GU toxicity at 5 years
Comparison vs partial nephrectomy in operable pts

Post-hoc analysis of metastasis-free survival
(MFS) and Eugonadal MFS in the RADIOSA phase
Il randomized trial
15-19 MAY 2026
STOCKHOLM, SWEDEN
ESTRO
2026
0000g
Background
Metastasis-free survival (MFS) has been validated as a surrogate endpoint for overall survival in non-metastatic prostate
cancer, and may represent an adaptable and clinically meaningful endpoint in oligorecurrent disease.
The phase II RADIOSA trial compared stereotactic body radiotherapy (SBRT) alone (Arm A) versus SBRT plus 6-month
androgen deprivation therapy (ADT, Arm B). This post-hoe analysis evaluated MFS and eugonadal MFS after
testosterone recovery, with a visual mapping of disease trajectories through a Sankey diagram.
Methods
A total of 102 patients were randomized 1:l to Arm A (SBRT) or Arm B (SBRT + ADT).
Median follow-up (Reverse KM) was 49.23 months (95% C1 42.47-54.8).
MFS was defined as the time between randomisation and the appearance of a metastatic recurrence (any MI) on imaging.
Eugonadal MFS was calculated from testosterone recovery to new metastasis or last follow-up.
Kaplan-Meier (KM) survival curves were compared with log-rank tests; hazard ratios were estimated using Cox models.

All patients enrolled in arm B, except two, reached
testosterone recovery within the follow-up period. After
testosterone recovery, eugonadal MFS
remained
significantly longer in Arm B (Figure 1b, p < 0.05).
confirming that the advantage of the combination persisted
beyond

Study aim
Given that a boost reduces the risk of IBTR by approximately 50%' and guided by the Assisi
thresholds?, the objective of our study is:
To determine 5-and 10-year IBTR rates in patients treated with or without a radiotherapy boost
To identify subgroups of patients in whom a boost may be omitted, i.e.
• Treated with a boost who have 10-year IBTR rates of <3%
• Treated without a boost who have 10-year IBTR rates of <6%

2026
Conclusions
• Low IBTR rates across all patient subgroups
• Based on the presence of combined risk factors, 5-and 10-year IBTR rates exceeded
the Assisi thresholds only in:
• Patients with ≥ 3 risk factors treated with a boost at 10 years
Take home messages
• The low IBTR rates across all subgroups suggest that
• In patients with 0-2 risk factors a tumour bed boost can be omitted, in the modern
systemic therapy era
• In patients with ≥ 3 risk factors the clinical relevance of a tumour bed boost remains
open to discussion
• Further research is needed to identify specific subgroups of patients who benefit from a
boost

Conclusion & Take-Home Message
5 fraction prostate and pelvic nodal (PPN) SBRT is feasible and safe in a
multicentre setting
Compared with prostate-only SBRT, PPN-SBRT increases GI side effects
in the short-term, but symptoms resolved quickly with no difference at 12
weeks
Differences in GI side effects were reflected in patient-reported outcomes
(EPIC-26 bowel domain) at 4 weeks
No differences in acute GU side effects (clinician or patient-reported)
were seen
Ongoing follow-up will provide data on late effects and cancer control

Background
Methods
• Some breast-cancer patients still develop locoregional or

and target-volume definition.

- In-volume: within CTV
- Marginal: outside CTV, 250% prescribed dose
- Out-of-volume: <50% prescribed dose
• Dosimetry: planned dose at recurrence site estimated on the
initial RT-plan CT scan

Background & Methods
Background
Moderately hypofractionated adjuvant whole-breast radiotherapy for early
node-negative breast cancer is standard worldwide, but long-term
randomised evidence remains limited. Here, we present the Danish
Breast Cancer Group HYPO trial prespecified 10-year outcomes.
Methods
• Phase III randomised non-inferiority trial (1:1) across Denmark,
Norway and Germany (2009-2014)
• 1,882 women with node-negative breast cancer or DCIS
• Whole-breast RT: 50 Gy/25 fractions vs 40 Gy/15 fractions
• Primary endpoint: 3-year grade 22 breast induration at two consequtive
visits, or at final follow-up
• Morbidity evaluated year 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 10
• These results are the prespecified 10-year analyses of toxicity.

Conclusion & Take-Home Message
40 Gy / 15 fractions reduced long-term breast
induration without compromising recurrence or
survival outcomes.
At 12.8 years follow-up, results showed:
• Lower fibrosis risk with maintained cancer outcomes
• Supports continued use of moderately hypofractionated
whole-breast radiotherapy
Less toxicity. Same cancer
-control.

• At 10 years, IBTR event incidence is low in this higher-than-average risk breast cancer
group, whether boost is delivered sequentially or simultaneously

estimate for control group at 5 years in original sample size calculations
• SIB is a safe treatment with reduced patient visits
• Further escalation of boost dose does not appear advantageous
• These results reaffirm use of 48Gy SIB in this population as standard of care

Discussion
• OLIGOMA is the first RCT to demonstrate a PFS-benefit from MDT in
patients with oligometastatic breast cancer.
• Majority had ER/PR pos. HER2 neg. disease in the first line-setting
• >80% of patients had 1-3 metastases, 2/3 of lesions were bone metastases.
• Short-term quality of life was not impaired by MDT.
• Recruitment was stopped early (< 20% of initial, < 40% of amended #)
• Numerous trials are still ongoing (incl. TAORMINA, STEREO-SEIN, LARA,
CLEAR, COSMO, ARCHER, ISTMET, OLIGAMI).
• Comprehensive analyses are needed to determine which patients benefit
most.

Conclusion & Take-Home Message
"ESTRO
2026
At 15 years median of follow up, the phase III APBI-IMRT Florence trial (NCT02104895)
demonstrates durable oncological outcomes with PB in selected patients with early breast cancer
Although a numerical increase in IBTR was observed, this was largely attributable to new
primary tumours rather than true local recurrences.
No differences were observed in loco-regional control, distant metastasis, breast cancer-
specific survival or overall survival.
These findings provide mature evidence supporting PBl as a safe de-escalation strategy and
reinforce its role as a standard treatment option in appropriately selected patients

SUMMARY
• POP-RT (2 years ADT) demonstrated significant benefit in bFFS, cFFS, and MFS with WPRT.
• PEACE-2 (3 years ADT, modern imaging, dose-escalated RT) showed no significant improvement in bPFS, MFS, CSS, or OS.
Only a borderline trend in cPFS (HR 0.81, p=0.09). No additional toxicity observed.
